---
title: "IS4010: AI-Enhanced Application Development"
subtitle: "Week 02"
author: "Brandon M. Greenwell"
format: 
  revealjs:
    self-contained: false
    theme: dracula
    transition: fade
---

# AI tools for programming

## Welcome to the AI revolution

::: {.incremental}
- Today we'll explore the landscape of AI programming tools
- You'll learn to work with AI as your **pair programming partner**
- We'll compare the major players and find the right tool for each job
- By the end, you'll understand how to "talk" to AI about code
:::

::: notes
Start with enthusiasm - this is genuinely exciting technology that will transform how students approach programming. Emphasize that we're not replacing programming skills, but augmenting them.

Demo hook: Have three browser tabs open (Gemini, ChatGPT, Claude) and be ready to show the same prompt to all three for the live comparison.
:::

---

## Course motto: 

> Augment, don't automate!

::: {.incremental}
- **AI is your co-pilot, not your autopilot**
- Think of AI as a brilliant but sometimes eccentric junior developer
- AI provides suggestions → You review, approve, and own the code
- **Your job**: Direct, verify, and integrate AI assistance
- **Remember**: The pilot is always responsible for the flight
:::

::: notes
This is the foundational philosophy for the entire course. Spend time here - students need to understand that they remain in control and responsible for all code they submit.

Use the pilot/co-pilot analogy throughout the course. It's memorable and accurate.
:::

---

## What are AI programming tools?

::: {.incremental}
- **Code generation**: Turn comments and prompts into working code
- **Code explanation**: Break down complex functions into plain English  
- **Debugging assistance**: Help identify and fix errors
- **Refactoring support**: Improve code structure and style
- **Learning accelerator**: Get instant feedback and alternative approaches
:::

---

## Under the hood: LLMs 🧠

::: {.incremental}
- **Large Language Models (LLMs)**: AI systems trained on massive amounts of text from the internet
- **Pattern recognition experts**: They've learned patterns in human language *and code*
- **Sophisticated autocomplete**: Like your phone's predictive text, but incredibly advanced
- **Not databases**: They don't "know" facts—they predict what text should come next
- **Surprisingly good at code**: Programming languages follow patterns, just like natural language
:::

::: notes
This is crucial foundational knowledge. Students often think AI "knows" things, when really it's predicting based on patterns.

Key analogies to use:
- **Autocomplete on steroids**: Everyone understands phone predictive text
- **Pattern matching**: Like learning to recognize handwriting, but for language/code patterns
- **Very sophisticated parrot**: Can produce remarkably coherent text without true "understanding"

Important points:
- LLMs were trained on billions of lines of code from GitHub and other sources
- They learned that certain patterns appear together (if you write a function definition, a return statement usually follows)
- This is why they're good at code completion but can make subtle logical errors
- They work the same way for Python, Rust, English, or any other language—it's all just patterns in text

Set expectations: These tools are incredibly useful but require human judgment and verification.
:::

---

## Four main categories:
::: {.incremental}
- **Conversational AI models**: General-purpose models (Gemini, ChatGPT, Claude)
- **Specialized code assistants**: Purpose-built for coding ([GitHub Copilot](https://github.com/features/copilot), [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code))
- **Command-line tools**: Terminal-based AI access ([Gemini CLI](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli), [Copilot CLI](https://github.com/features/copilot/cli))
- **Code analysis tools**: Project-wide review and feedback ([Google Jules](https://jules.google/))
:::

::: notes
Frame these as the five core use cases students will encounter most frequently. Every AI interaction should fit into one of these categories.

Ask students: "Which of these sounds most useful to you right now?" Get a sense of where they are in their programming journey.
:::

---

## Conversational AI models 🤖

**The big three:** [Gemini](https://gemini.google.com/), [ChatGPT](https://chat.openai.com/), and [Claude](https://claude.ai/) (These are general-purpose AI models that can help with programming.)

::: {.incremental}
- **All offer free tiers** - Cost shouldn't be a barrier!
- **Strong programming support** - Excellent for [Python](https://www.python.org/), explanations, and debugging
- **Different personalities** - Each has unique strengths
- **Web-based interfaces** - Easy to access
- **Great for learning** - Perfect for explanations, brainstorming, and code review
:::

::: notes
Emphasize that these are general-purpose AI models, not specialized coding tools. They're excellent for learning, explanations, and brainstorming, but they're different from purpose-built code assistants.

All three have free tiers - cost shouldn't be a barrier for students. Each has different strengths, and we'll see this in the live demo.

Point out that we're focusing on the web interfaces first - this is the easiest entry point for beginners.
:::

---

## Conversational coding 💬

**The CPTF framework for great prompts:**

::: {.incremental}
1. **Context**: "Here is my [Python](https://www.python.org/) function..."
2. **Persona**: "Act as a senior software developer reviewing my code."  
3. **Task**: "Find any potential bugs and suggest improvements."
4. **Format**: "Provide your answer as a numbered list."
:::

::: notes
This is crucial - the quality of AI responses depends entirely on prompt quality. Students often start with vague prompts like "fix my code" and get frustrated with the results.

Have a few examples ready:
- Bad: "Make this better"
- Good: "Act as a [Python](https://www.python.org/) expert. Here's my function that calculates fibonacci numbers. Explain how it works and suggest any performance improvements. Format your response with explanations and code examples."
:::

---

## Five essential prompt patterns

### 1. **Explain this code**
```
"Explain this Python dictionary comprehension step by step:

{word: len(word) for word in text.split() if len(word) > 3}"
```

### 2. **Generate boilerplate**
```
"Write a Numpy-style docstring for this function that calculates the area of a circle given its radius."
```

### 3. **Refactor for clarity**
```  
"Convert this for loop into a more Pythonic equivalent using list comprehension or built-in functions."
```

::: notes
Walk through each pattern with a concrete example. These are the patterns students will use 80% of the time.

Emphasize that good prompts are specific and provide context. The AI needs to understand what you're trying to accomplish.
:::

---

## More essential patterns

### 4. **Debug assistance**
```
"I'm getting a 'TypeError: unsupported operand type(s)' on line 15. Here's my code... What's the likely cause and how do I fix it?"
```

### 5. **Architecture advice**
```
"I need to store student information (name, ID, grades). Should I use a list of dictionaries or create a Student class? Explain the pros and cons of each approach."
```

::: notes
Pattern 4 is incredibly valuable - students often get cryptic error messages and spend hours debugging what an AI can spot in seconds.

Pattern 5 shows how AI can help with design decisions, not just code generation. This is where the "senior developer" persona really shines.
:::

---

## Live demo: Head-to-head comparison 🥊

**Our challenge**: Create a [Python](https://www.python.org/) function that takes a filename and returns the number of lines in the file, handling potential `FileNotFoundError` exceptions.

Let's give the **exact same prompt** to all three AIs:

> "Write a [Python](https://www.python.org/) function called `count_lines` that takes a filename as a parameter and returns the number of lines in the file. Handle the case where the file doesn't exist by returning 0. Include a [NumPy](https://numpy.org/)-style docstring."

::: notes
This is the highlight of the session! Have three browser tabs ready:
1. Gemini (gemini.google.com)
2. ChatGPT (chat.openai.com) 
3. Claude (claude.ai)

Paste the exact same prompt into each and show the results side-by-side. Point out:
- Different coding styles
- Quality of docstrings  
- Error handling approaches
- Explanation quality
- Any unique insights each model provides

This demonstrates that models have different "personalities" and there's no single "best" tool.
:::

---

## What did we learn? 🧠

::: {.incremental}
- **Different models, different strengths**: Each AI has its own "personality"
- **Quality varies**: Same prompt can yield different code quality
- **No single winner**: The "best" AI depends on your specific task
- **Explanation matters**: Some models explain their reasoning better
- **Style differences**: Notice variations in variable naming, comments, structure
:::

::: notes
After the live demo, take a few minutes to discuss what students observed. Ask:
- Which response did you prefer and why?
- Did any approach surprise you?
- What differences in coding style did you notice?

This reinforces that AI tools are aids to human judgment, not replacements for it.
:::

---

## Ethics and limitations ⚠️

::: {.incremental}
- **AI can hallucinate**: Models sometimes generate plausible-looking but incorrect code
- **You own the code**: Never submit code you don't understand
- **Privacy matters**: Be cautious about sharing sensitive info
- **Academic integrity**: Document your AI usage in assignments
- **Keep learning**: AI should accelerate your learning, not replace it
:::

::: notes
This is critical. Students need to understand that AI is a powerful tool but requires human oversight.

Share examples of AI hallucinations if you have them - perhaps a function that looks correct but has subtle bugs, or advice that sounds authoritative but is outdated.

Emphasize the course AI policy: use AI freely, but understand everything you submit.
:::

---

## A quick note on docstrings 📝

::: {.incremental}
- Before our lab, we need to introduce a key concept: the **docstring**
- A docstring is a string literal, enclosed in triple quotes (`"""`), that occurs as the first statement in a module, function, class, or method definition
- Its purpose is to explain what the code does
- We are learning about them now because they are the primary way you will communicate your intent to AI assistants
- **A good docstring leads to good AI-generated code**
:::

::: notes
This bridges from the AI discussion to the practical lab work. Students need to understand docstrings before Lab 02 Part 1.

Emphasize the connection: clear human communication (docstrings) leads to better AI assistance. This reinforces that AI tools respond to human intent and clarity.
:::

---

## Our docstring style: [NumPy](https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html)

::: {.incremental}
- There are several popular styles for writing docstrings. In this course, we will use the **[NumPy style](https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html)**
- It is clean, readable, and works very well with automated documentation tools
- See next slide for an example
:::

---

An example of Numpy-style docstrings:

```python
def add(num1, num2):
    """
    Add two numbers together.

    Parameters
    ----------
    num1 : int
        The first number.
    num2 : int
        The second number.

    Returns
    -------
    int
        The sum of the two numbers.
    """
    return num1 + num2
```

::: notes
Show the [NumPy](https://numpy.org/) docstring format that will be used throughout the course. This is important for Lab 02 where students will be working with docstrings extensively.

Point out:
- The brief description first
- Parameters section with types and descriptions  
- Returns section with type and description
- Clear, consistent formatting

This style works particularly well with AI tools because it provides structured, detailed context about function intent.
:::

---

## Introducing Lab 02 (Part 1) 🧪

- Your next lab assignment will give you hands-on practice with both AI tool types
- **Part 1**: "Copilot driving school" - Use **[GitHub Copilot](https://github.com/features/copilot) specifically**
- **Objective**: You will be given [Python](https://www.python.org/) function stubs with [NumPy](https://numpy.org/)-style docstrings. Let Copilot generate the implementations based on your clear documentation

---

# AI in your editor & beyond

## From browser to IDE

Why integration matters 🔗

::: {.incremental}
- **Stay in your flow state**: No context switching!
- **Keep your hands on the keyboard**: Faster than copy-paste workflows
- **Context awareness**: AI can see your entire project
- **Real-time assistance**: Get help as you type
- **Professional workflow**: This is how AI is used in the industry
:::

::: notes
Open with the value proposition - students should understand why we're moving from the browser-based tools from last session to integrated development tools.

Emphasize "flow state" - the psychological concept of being fully immersed in coding. Context switching breaks flow and reduces productivity.

Make sure students have [VS Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/) open and ready for the [GitHub Copilot](https://github.com/features/copilot) demonstration.
:::

---

## What's [GitHub Copilot](https://github.com/features/copilot)?

::: {.incremental}
- Purpose-built for coding and integrates directly into [VS Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/)!
- Trained specifically on code repositories from GitHub
- Provides **two main modes** of assistance:
  - **Inline suggestions**: "Ghost text" appears as you type
  - **Copilot Chat**: Conversational interface within your editor
- **Context-aware**: Sees your current file, open files, and project structure
:::

::: notes
This is the primary specialized code assistant for the course. Make sure students understand that Copilot is fundamentally different from the conversational AI models - it's purpose-built for code generation and editing.

Key point: Context awareness and integration. Copilot can see variable names, function signatures, imports, and other files in your project. This makes its suggestions much more relevant than general-purpose AI models working with isolated code snippets.
:::

---

## The magic of inline suggestions ✨

**How it works:**

::: {.incremental}
- Write a **descriptive comment** about what you want to accomplish
- Start coding...
- Copilot appears as **gray "ghost text"**
- Press **Tab** to accept, **Esc** to reject
- Use **Alt + ]** (Windows) or **Option + ]** (Mac) to cycle through alternatives
:::

---

## The magic of inline suggestions ✨

**Best practices:**

::: {.incremental}
- **Clear comments are key**: 

  `# Calculate the factorial of a positive integer`

- **Descriptive function names**: 

  `calculate_factorial` not `calc_fact`

- **Type hints help**: 

  `def factorial(n: int) -> int:`
:::

::: notes
This is the "classic" Copilot experience that most people think of. Prepare a live demo:

1. Open a new [Python](https://www.python.org/) file
2. Write a comment: "# A function that calculates the factorial of a number"
3. Start typing: "def factorial(n: int) -> int:"
4. Let Copilot complete the function
5. Show how to cycle through alternatives

Emphasize that the quality of suggestions depends heavily on the quality of your comments and function names.
:::

---

## Copilot Chat

**Your conversational partner** 💬

Three powerful modes:

::: {.incremental}
- **Planning mode**: Architectural decisions and project structure
- **Ask mode**: Questions, explanations, and debugging help
- **Agent mode**: Autonomous task completion and complex operations
:::

---

## Copilot Chat

**Your conversational partner** 💬

When to use each mode:

::: {.incremental}
- **Planning**: "How should I structure a student gradebook application?"
- **Ask**: "Explain this regex pattern" or "Why am I getting this error?"
- **Agent**: "Refactor this class to use data classes" or "Add error handling to all functions"
:::

::: notes
This is new content that distinguishes between different interaction patterns. Students need to understand when to use each mode:

**Planning mode**: High-level architectural decisions. Use when you're not sure how to approach a problem.

**Ask mode**: Specific questions about existing code. Use when you need explanations or help debugging.

**Agent mode**: When you want the AI to perform specific, well-defined tasks autonomously. Use when you know what needs to be done but want AI to do the implementation.

Prepare examples for each mode to show during the demo.
:::

---

## Live demo 🎬

**Building a [Python](https://www.python.org/) utility with AI assistance:**

::: {.incremental}
1. **Start with a comment**: `# A function that calculates compound interest`
2. **Let Copilot generate the function**: Accept or refine the suggestion
3. **Use Chat to enhance**:
   - `/explain` the generated code
   - "Add a [NumPy](https://numpy.org/)-style docstring and type hints"
   - `/tests` to generate [pytest](https://pytest.org/) unit tests
4. **Refactor and debug**: "Make this function more robust" or "Find the bug in this code"
:::

::: notes
This is the centerpiece demonstration. Have a live coding session where you build a small utility from scratch using only AI assistance. Suggested function: compound interest calculator.

Key moments to highlight:
1. How inline suggestions save typing
2. How chat can explain unfamiliar code
3. The "wow" moment when `/tests` generates complete unit tests
4. How to iteratively improve code with AI feedback

If something goes wrong (AI gives bad suggestions), use it as a teaching moment about verification and human oversight.
:::

---

## Command-line AI assistants 💻

**Why use terminal-based AI?**

::: {.incremental}
- **Stay in your workflow**: No need to switch between terminal and browser
- **Quick queries**: Fast answers without leaving the command line
- **Scriptable**: Can be integrated into automated workflows
- **Code context**: Easy to pipe file contents directly to AI
- **Privacy options**: Some tools offer better data handling
:::

::: notes
Set up the value proposition for command-line tools. These are for "power users" who want to integrate AI into their terminal workflow.

Students may ask: "Why not just use the browser?" Answer: When you're deep in terminal work (git operations, file manipulation, debugging), switching to a browser breaks your flow.
:::

---

## [Gemini CLI](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli)

### (Free) AI in your terminal 🖥️

**Common usage patterns:**

```bash
# Start an interactive gemini session
gemini

# Quick code questions
gemini "How do I install [pytest](https://pytest.org/) in [Python](https://www.python.org/)?"

# Code generation
gemini "Write a [Rust](https://www.rust-lang.org/) function to read a CSV file"

# Debugging help
gemini "Explain this error: ImportError: No module named 'requests'"
```

::: notes
Prepare a live demo of Gemini CLI. Have it installed and configured beforehand.

Show practical examples:
1. Quick package installation help
2. Language translation ([Python](https://www.python.org/) to [Rust](https://www.rust-lang.org/))
3. Error message explanation
4. Code review of a file: `cat script.py | gemini "Review this code for bugs"`

Mention that students can get a free API key from [Google AI Studio](https://aistudio.google.com/).
:::

---

## [GitHub Copilot CLI](https://github.com/features/copilot/cli)

### (Free tier) Natural language terminal ⌨️

**Common usage patterns:**

```bash
# Ask how to do something in the terminal
gh copilot suggest "list all python files larger than 1MB"

# Explain a complex command
gh copilot explain "find . -name '*.py' -size +1M"

# Execute suggestions directly
# (Follow the prompts to execute or copy)
```

::: notes
GitHub Copilot CLI is part of the `gh` (GitHub CLI) tool. It's excellent for when you know *what* you want to do in the terminal but forget the exact flags or syntax.

Key feature: It doesn't just give you code; it gives you terminal commands and explains what they do before you run them.

Students with the GitHub Student Developer Pack get this as part of their Copilot access.
:::

---

## [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

### (Not free) AI in your terminal 🖥️

**What is it?**

::: {.incremental}
- **Anthropic's premium CLI tool** for professional development
- **Advanced features**: Project context, file editing, test generation, and much, much more!
- **Industry adoption**: Popular in professional development
- **Subscription required**: Currently better than free alternatives
:::

---

## [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/code)

### (Not free) AI in your terminal 🖥️

**Why mention it?**

::: {.incremental}
- **Career awareness**: You'll likely encounter it in industry
- **Feature preview**: Shows where AI coding tools are heading
- **Upgrade path**: When you outgrow free tools, this is an option
:::

::: notes
We're not doing a live demo since it's a paid tool, but students should be aware it exists.

Position this as "what you might use in your first job" - many companies are adopting premium AI tools for their development teams.

Key message: Start with free tools (Gemini, Copilot free tier), but be aware of the premium tools available for career purposes.
:::

---

## [Google Jules](https://jules.google/)

**AI-powered code review** 🔍

Revolutionary approach:

::: {.incremental}
- **Understands entire codebases**, not just individual files
- **Comprehensive analysis**: Security, performance, maintainability
- **Context-aware suggestions**: Considers your project's architecture
- **Educational feedback**: Explains *why* changes are recommended
:::

---

## [Google Jules](https://jules.google/)

**AI-powered code review** 🔍

Good for:

::: {.incremental}
- **Code review**: Before submitting assignments or pull requests
- **Learning**: Understanding best practices in real projects
- **Debugging**: Finding subtle issues across multiple files
- **Refactoring**: Identifying improvement opportunities
- **Addressing [PRs](https://docs.github.com/en/pull-requests/collaborating-with-pull-requests/proposing-changes-to-your-work-with-pull-requests/about-pull-requests)**: [Example](https://github.com/bgreenwell/statlingua/pull/7)
:::

::: notes
Jules represents a new category of AI tools - those that can understand and analyze entire projects, not just individual functions.

Prepare a demo with a small [Python](https://www.python.org/) project (maybe 3-4 files). Upload it to [Jules](https://jules.google/) and show:
1. How it analyzes code structure
2. Security and performance recommendations
3. Suggestions for code organization
4. Educational explanations of why changes are recommended

Emphasize that this is perfect for their lab submissions - they can get AI feedback before turning in their work.
:::

---

## Choosing your AI toolkit 🧰

**Required for this course (free):**

- [GitHub Copilot](https://github.com/features/copilot) 
- [Gemini CLI](https://github.com/google-gemini/gemini-cli) 

**Recommended for this course (also free):**

- [GitHub Copilot CLI](https://github.com/features/copilot/cli)
- [Gemini web interface](https://gemini.google.com/) 
- [Google Jules](https://jules.google/)

::: notes
Give clear guidance on what students need vs. what's nice to have.

[GitHub Copilot](https://github.com/features/copilot) is required because it integrates directly with [VS Code](https://code.visualstudio.com/) and provides the best learning experience for the course.

Everything else is optional but recommended for exploration. Encourage students to try different tools and find their preferences.

Remind them that many tools offer free tiers or student discounts.
:::

---

## Your professional AI workflow 🔄

**Daily development:**

1. **Start with comments**: Describe your intent clearly
2. **Use Copilot inline**: Accept, modify, or reject suggestions
3. **Chat for complex problems**: Switch to conversational help when stuck
4. **Command-line for quick help**: Terminal-based queries for efficiency

---

## Your professional AI workflow 🔄

**Code review and submission:**

1. **Jules analysis**: Upload complete projects for comprehensive review
2. **AI-assisted debugging**: Use chat to identify and fix issues
3. **Documentation**: Generate docstrings and comments with AI help
4. **Testing**: Auto-generate test cases with `/tests` command

::: notes
This gives students a complete workflow they can start using immediately.

Emphasize the progression: start with simple inline suggestions, graduate to conversational help for complex problems, and use specialized tools for specific tasks like code review.

The key insight: different tools for different phases of development. You wouldn't use the same tool for initial brainstorming as you would for final code review.
:::

---

## Remember: You are the pilot ✈️

::: {.incremental}
- **AI provides suggestions** → You make decisions
- **AI explains possibilities** → You choose the best approach
- **AI generates code** → You understand and verify it
- **AI finds patterns** → You apply domain knowledge
- **Always maintain responsibility** for the final product
:::

**The golden rule**: Never submit code you don't understand!

::: notes
End with the core philosophy. Students should leave feeling empowered, not dependent.

The goal is to make students better programmers faster, not to replace their thinking. AI should accelerate learning and remove tedious barriers, but students must remain intellectually engaged.

Ask: "What questions do you have about integrating AI into your development workflow?"
:::

---

## Introducing Lab 02 (Part 2) 🚀

- **Part 2**: "Prompt engineering challenge" - Use **any conversational AI model**
- **Your choice**: [Gemini](https://gemini.google.com/), [ChatGPT](https://chat.openai.com/), [Claude](https://claude.ai/), or others
- **Objective**: Debug, refactor, and document buggy [Python](https://www.python.org/) code snippets by crafting high-quality, effective prompts using the CPTF method
- All instructions will be in `week02/lab02.md`

---

## Time for Lab 02! 🧪

- Your next hands-on lab: AI-assisted [Python](https://www.python.org/) development
- **Part 1**: "Copilot driving school" - hands-on practice with inline suggestions
- **Part 2**: "Prompt engineering challenge" - master conversational AI debugging
- Please navigate to [`week02/lab02.md`](https://github.com/bgreenwell/is4010-course/blob/main/week02/lab02.md) in the course repository for step-by-step instructions
